insights.title: Mentor Synthesis: Experimentation + Clarification — The Two Sides of Agent Mastery
insights.key_insight:
Two talks captured on the same day reveal two complementary halves of what it means to work effectively with AI coding agents:

**Karpathy** identifies the *failure mode*: agents assume, don't ask, don't manage confusion, and produce bloated code. His fix is a *principle* — "Think Before Coding": force the agent to state assumptions, ask when uncertain, present tradeoffs.

**Mihail Eric** identifies the *process gap*: no one has best practices yet. Even Anthropic is experimenting in real-time. His fix is a *practice* — build systematic experimentation into your workflow. Try tools, measure results, iterate.

Together: **Principle tells you what to optimize for. Process tells you how to discover the right implementation for your context.**

insights.title: Mentoring session — Whiteboard-reveal PR review
insights.through_line:
Warren's friction is not simply that AI-written PRs may be wrong. The harder cost is post-hoc comprehension: after the AI has implemented a change, he has to manually reconstruct the codebase context, intended design, file relationships, and correctness argument.

This connects Naval's "AI factory" idea with the earlier human/agent boundary theme. The useful factory is not only code generation. It is a reviewable context-and-change loop that helps Warren understand unfamiliar codebases faster while still owning judgment.

insights.title: Mentor Synthesis: The Thinking-Tool Boundary
insights.key_insight:
Three speakers, same diagnosis from three different angles:

| Speaker | Talk | Core Warning |
|---------|------|-------------|
| Karpathy | CLAUDE.md / Think Before Coding | Agents make wrong assumptions silently. Force them to ask first. |
| Eric | Writing Code → Managing Agents | Build experimentation into your workflow. Nobody has this figured out. |
| Horthy | Everything Wrong With RPI | Outsourcing thinking to agents = 50% more output, 50% cleanup. |

They're all describing the same boundary: **where human thinking stops and agent execution begins.** Get the boundary wrong — put it too far toward the agent — and you get bloated code, wrong assumptions, cleanup debt. Get it right — keep ownership of the architecture, force assumption-surfacing, iterate — and the agent becomes an amplifier.

---

insights.practice_nudge: Add a "next step" career progression task at the end of each mentoring session — a small, concrete thing to try in spare time that connects the discussion to practice (e.g., a small Docker task, a proof-led decomposition drill).
